original_abstract: The terms *fundamentalist* and *fundamentalism* emerged in 1920 within the Northern Baptist Convention, a period marked by intense theological upheaval as American Protestant denominations grappled with the rise of modernism. This article argues that these terms were originally embraced as positive identifiers by defenders of orthodoxy. However, almost immediately following their inception, they drew sharp criticism from more liberal Christian factions. Like many theological concepts, these neologisms underwent significant semantic shifts, specifically pejoration and lexical extension. By 1923, the term *fundamentalist* had already bled into political journalism to describe rigid adherence to any particular ideology. Yet the most definitive transformation occurred during the heavily publicized Scopes "monkey trial" of 1925. It was this event that cemented the terms in the public consciousness across North America and the United Kingdom, forever linking fundamentalism with anti-intellectualism and obscurantism.
humanized_abstract: The terms fundamentalist and fundamentalism were introduced in 1920 in the Northern Baptist Convention, an ecclesiastical body that comprised a segment of American Protestant denominations at war with modernism. Arguing that these terms were introduced as "flags of honour" by advocates of orthodoxy, the author maintains that they were soon castigated by liberals, undergoing what linguists call pejoration and lexical extension. By 1923, fundamentalist was also applied in the political journalism of the day to describe "the sticking to some creed, set of views or theory even when it is discredited" by the public at large. But the defining moment that shaped the currency of the terms in the north American and British minds of the common man occurred at the notoriously publicised Scopes "monkey trial" of 1925.

By 1923, fundamentalist was also applied in the political journalism of the day to describe "the sticking to some creed, set of views or theory even when it is discredited" by the public at large. But the defining moment that shaped the currency of the terms in the north American and British minds of the common man occurred at the notoriously publicised Scopes "monkey trial" of 1925.
